data.output:
1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51	
52	### docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md
53	- Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate.
54	- Problem: add "no provenance for generated artifacts."
55	- Goals: reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add "100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP."
56	- Non-goals: sharpen loop wording (mechanical drain in scope; supervisory content-aware decisions out). Add: "DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers."
57	- FEAT-001/005/006/007/008/010/019/021 blurbs updated.
58	
59	### FEAT-001-cli.md
60	- Promote `run` / `try` / `work` to top-level.
61	- `ddx agent` mounts upstream Cobra root structurally; no DDx-defined leaf subcommands beneath it.
62	- Hard-deprecation handlers for `ddx agent {run, execute-bead, execute-loop}`.
63	- New namespaces: `ddx runs` (cross-layer evidence introspection), `ddx tries` (layer-2 specifically), `ddx work workers` (layer-3 worker management).
64	- Remove FEAT-001:92 backward-compatibility clause for `ddx agent execute-loop`.
65	
66	### FEAT-005-artifacts.md
67	- Identity broadens to non-markdown via sidecar `.ddx.yaml`.
68	- Add `media_type` and `generated_by` fields.
69	- Authority rule documented: identity present → artifact.
70	- Terminology cleanup ("identity," not "ddx: identity").
71	
72	### FEAT-006-agent-service.md
73	- Tighten to layer-1 consumer-side wrapper that powers `ddx run`.
74	- `ddx agent` is structural passthrough (mount upstream Cobra root).
75	- Non-bead Profile/Permissions selection (artifact-keyed path).
76	- Migration table: what stays in FEAT-006 (CONTRACT-003 boundary, profile/permissions), what moves to FEAT-010 (worktree, merge/preserve, evidence bundles, queue drain), what moves to FEAT-001 (CLI surface).
77	- Session-log boundary clarified: DDx owns the envelope/pointer in evidence bundles; upstream owns inner log shape.
78	
79	### FEAT-010-executions.md (substantial refactor)
80	- Three-layer architecture explicit and load-bearing.
81	- Substrate unification: one record shape; layer metadata.
82	- Migrate `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` to one layout.
83	- Specify `ddx work` no-progress / stop conditions.
84	- Narrow read-only amendment for `artifactRegenerate` only.
85	- No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.
86	
87	### FEAT-007-doc-graph.md
88	- Sidecar scanner refactor; `media_type`; `generated_by` edge with separate staleness rule (provenance, not authority — does not cascade like `depends_on`); version bump on Complete-status feature.
89	- 100% read endpoints for graph state, generated-artifact metadata, sidecar content.
90	
91	### FEAT-008-web-ui.md
92	- Media-type-aware rendering (markdown, mermaid SVG, excalidraw embed, image inline, PDF embed).
93	- Regenerate action wired to `artifactRegenerate` mutation; reconcile with existing FEAT-008:391 weak Re-run requirement.
94	- Layer-aware run views (`work` → `try` → `run` drill-down).
95	
96	### FEAT-019-agent-evaluation.md
97	- Contract to evaluation UX (comparison views, grading rubric storage and display, benchmark result aggregation).
98	- Workflow shapes → skills library.
99	- Remove peer-to-FEAT-010 assertions (FEAT-019:40, :404).
100	
101	### FEAT-021-dashboard-ui.md
102	- Layer-aware run-history routes against unified substrate.
103	
104	### FEAT-014, FEAT-013
105	- One-sentence touches: cost attribution and concurrency for generate-artifact.
106	
107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`
113	- `agent_capabilities.go`
114	- `agent_doctor.go`
115	- `agent_check.go`
116	- `agent_models.go`
117	- `agent_providers.go`
118	- `agent_route_status.go`
119	- `agent_catalog.go`
120	- `agent_reindex.go`
121	- `agent_benchmark.go` (replaced by `benchmark-suite` skill)
122	- `agent_replay.go` (replaced by `replay-bead` skill)
123	
124	**Move to other DDx namespaces (DDx-only concerns):**
125	- `agent_executions.go` → `tries_*.go`
126	- `agent_log.go` → `runs_log.go`
127	- `agent_metrics.go` → `runs_metrics.go`
128	- `agent_usage.go` → folded into `runs_metrics.go`
129	- `agent_workers.go` → `work_workers.go`
130	
131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).
134	
135	**`agent_cmd.go` itself thins to:** mount upstream Cobra root + register DDx overrides.
136	
137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
140	
141	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation (replaces `--quorum` flag and `agent benchmark`)
142	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions, baseline diff (replaces `agent replay`)
143	- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix
144	- `adversarial-review`
145	- `effort-estimate`
146	- `bead-breakdown`
147	
148	## Bead breakdown
149	
150	**Frame updates (sequential where dependencies require):**
151	
152	1. Update product-vision.md (+ three-layer subsection)
153	2. Update prd.md (+ three-layer goal, sharpened loop non-goal, no-catalog non-goal)
154	3. Update FEAT-001 (CLI surface refactor; remove backcompat clause; new namespaces) — **hard predecessor for #6, #7**
155	4. Update FEAT-005 (multi-media identity refactor)
156	5. CONTRACT-003 audit — confirm `ddx-agent` exports a mountable Cobra root and other gaps
157	6. *(Conditional)* Open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent` if #5 surfaces gaps
158	7. Update FEAT-006 (layer-1 consumer; structural passthrough; non-bead Profile selection; migration table; session-log envelope boundary)
159	8. Refactor FEAT-010 (three-layer architecture; substrate unification; on-disk migration; no-progress stop conditions; narrow `artifactRegenerate` amendment)
160	9. Update FEAT-007 (sidecar refactor; media_type; generated_by; separate staleness; version bump)
161	10. Contract FEAT-019 to evaluation UX
162	11. Update FEAT-008 (media rendering, regenerate action, layer-aware run views)
163	12. Update FEAT-021 (layer-aware run-history routes)
164	13. Update FEAT-014 (cost attribution for generate-artifact)
165	14. Update FEAT-013 (concurrency note for regenerate)
166	15. **Repo-wide audit & verb migration** — atomic with #3:
167	    - Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands (list above).
168	    - Migrate DDx-only run/evidence subcommands to `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`.
169	    - Register DDx overrides on the mounted subtree (deprecations + condense).
170	    - Delete `agent_reindex.go` outright.
171	    - Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to new verbs.
172	
173	**Design artifacts (concurrent with frame):**
174	
175	16. Architecture: artifact identity schema (concurrent with #4)
176	17. Architecture: graph schema with media_type + generated_by + separate staleness (concurrent with #9)
177	18. Architecture: three-layer run substrate — record shape, layer metadata, on-disk layout, migration, `ddx work` stop conditions (concurrent with #8)
178	19. Sidecar `.ddx.yaml` schema design
179	
180	**Read-coverage thread:**
181	
182	20. Read-coverage audit — enumerate every CLI-visible read; map to HTTP/MCP coverage; identify gaps
183	21. Per-gap beads — sequenced by user-visibility impact, land case-by-case (this is a pointer to ongoing work, not a single bead)
184	
185	**Skill library:**
186	
187	22. `compare-prompts` skill
188	23. `replay-bead` skill
189	24. `benchmark-suite` skill
190	25. `adversarial-review` skill
191	26. `effort-estimate` skill
192	27. `bead-breakdown` skill
193	
194	**Implementation (P2, after frame & design):**
195	
196	- CLI: `ddx run` / `ddx try` top-level commands; mount upstream agent Cobra root; deprecation handlers
197	- CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)
198	- CLI: `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`, `ddx artifact list --media-type <t>`
199	- Storage: sidecar reader/writer; size-guard threshold/warning/tests
200	- Graph: `generated_by` edges + provenance staleness rule
201	- Run framework: refactor to one substrate per #18; generator metadata; `ddx work` no-progress detection
202	- Server: `artifactRegenerate` write endpoint
203	- Server UI: media-type renderers; regenerate action; layer-aware run history
204	- Migration: passive `media_type` only (read-time inference from extension); on-disk run-record migration per #18
205	
206	## Constraints
207	
208	- Beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — old verbs are referenced in scripts/library/personas/init help text/CI; an isolated #3 break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced this plan.
209	- Bead #5 (CONTRACT-003 audit) gates #7 (FEAT-006). #6 conditional.
210	- Architecture sketches (#16–#19) run concurrent with their corresponding frame beads, not after — keeps frame/design from drifting.
211	- Read-coverage epic (#20–#21) is independent and not gated on this plan.
212	
213	## Refinement provenance
214	
215	Refined through 3 rounds of adversarial review (codex via `ddx agent run --harness codex` + fresh-eyes general-purpose subagent in parallel) plus user-resolved questions Q1–Q12. Key user decisions: artifact authority (Q1), FEAT-019 child-of-010 (Q2), reads-matter-writes-case-by-case (Q3), check-in unconditionally (Q4), CONTRACT-003 amendments allowed (Q5), substrate unification (Q6), no run-type catalog (Q7), no v1-gate framing (Q8), FEAT-019 contracts (Q9), generate-artifact split layer-1/layer-2 (Q10), quorum-as-skill (Q11), structural passthrough not per-command (Q12 follow-up).
216

replay-bead
SKILL.md

1	---
2	name: compare-prompts
3	description: Dispatch the same prompt across N agent invocations (different harnesses, models, personas, or prompt variants) and aggregate the results into a single comparison report. Use when you need to benchmark prompt variants, run a quorum-style consensus check, compare model outputs side-by-side, or replay a prompt under altered conditions. Replaces the deprecated `--quorum` flag and `ddx agent benchmark` CLI.
4	---
5	
6	# Compare Prompts
7	
8	A workflow skill for N-arm dispatch and result aggregation. Composes
9	`ddx agent run` (the layer-1 invocation primitive) into a comparison
10	matrix: same prompt, varied conditions, structured aggregation.
11	
12	This skill replaces two retired surfaces:
13	
14	- `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy>` — the inline quorum flag.
15	- `ddx agent benchmark` — the per-prompt benchmark CLI.
16	
17	Both collapse into the same workflow shape: dispatch N invocations,
18	aggregate their outputs, render a verdict.
19	
20	## When to use
21	
22	- **Prompt benchmarking** — compare two prompt variants on the same
23	  task to decide which one ships.
24	- **Model comparison** — same prompt across `claude` / `codex` /
25	  `gemini` (or different models within one harness) to see where
26	  outputs diverge.
27	- **Consensus checks (quorum)** — run a review prompt across N
28	  harnesses and require majority/unanimous agreement before acting.
29	- **Persona comparison** — same prompt under different personas to
30	  see how role framing changes the answer.
31	- **Replay under altered conditions** — re-run a prior prompt with a
32	  changed model, profile, or context to A/B a hypothesis.
33	
34	For pressure-testing a plan or spec with adversarial framing, prefer
35	the `adversarial-review` skill — it layers a critic-only prompt
36	contract on top of this same N-arm shape.
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### 1. Define the comparison axis
41	
42	Pick exactly one axis to vary. Mixing axes makes results
43	uninterpretable.
44	
45	| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| harness | `--harness claude`, `--harness codex`, `--harness gemini` | prompt, profile, persona |
48	| model | `--model <a>`, `--model <b>` | harness, prompt, persona |
49	| prompt | prompt-v1.md, prompt-v2.md | harness, model, persona |
50	| persona | `--persona reviewer`, `--persona architect` | harness, prompt, model |
51	| profile | `--profile cheap`, `--profile smart` | harness, prompt, persona |
52	
53	If you must vary two axes (e.g., harness × model), enumerate the full
54	matrix explicitly and label each arm.
55	
56	### 2. Author the prompt
57	
58	Write a single prompt file. Include an **output contract** so each
59	arm produces structurally comparable output:
60	
61	```markdown
62	## Task
63	
64	<the actual ask — code review, plan critique, design question, etc.>
65	
66	## Output contract
67	
68	Produce your answer as:
69	
70	### Verdict: <one line>
71	
72	### Reasoning
73	<2–6 sentences>
74	
75	### Specific findings
76	- <finding 1>
77	- <finding 2>
78	```
79	
80	Without an output contract, aggregation degenerates into freeform
81	prose comparison. The contract is what makes N-arm dispatch
82	mechanically aggregateable.
83	
84	### 3. Dispatch the arms in parallel
85	
86	Run each arm as a backgrounded `ddx agent run`, capturing stdout
87	to a per-arm file. Wait for all arms before aggregating.
88	
89	```bash
90	PROMPT=prompt.md
91	OUT=compare-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
92	mkdir -p "$OUT"
93	
94	# Three-arm harness comparison
95	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/claude.md" 2> "$OUT/claude.err" &
96	ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/codex.md"  2> "$OUT/codex.err"  &
97	ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/gemini.md" 2> "$OUT/gemini.err" &
98	wait
99	```
100	
101	Per-arm conventions:
102	
103	- One file per arm, named for the varied axis value (`claude.md`,
104	  `codex.md`, …) — not `arm1.md` / `arm2.md`.
105	- Capture stderr separately. Failures in one arm should not silently
106	  drop a result row from the aggregate.
107	- Tag arms with the full invocation in a header comment when the
108	  filename alone doesn't disambiguate (e.g., model+persona variants).
109	
110	### 4. Verify each arm produced output
111	
112	Before aggregating, confirm each arm exited cleanly and produced
113	output that satisfies the prompt's output contract. An arm that
114	errored or returned empty must be marked as such — never silently
115	omitted.
116	
117	```bash
118	for arm in "$OUT"/*.md; do
119	  if [ ! -s "$arm" ]; then
120	    echo "ARM FAILED: $arm"
121	  fi
122	done
123	```
124	
125	### 5. Aggregate
126	
127	Aggregate per the comparison axis. The aggregation policy depends on
128	the use case:
129	
130	**Quorum (consensus check):**
131	
132	- `unanimous` — every arm must produce the same verdict.
133	- `majority` — strictly more than half the arms agree.
134	- `any` — at least one arm produced the verdict (lowest bar).
135	
136	Surface disagreement explicitly. If 2 of 3 arms say APPROVE and 1
137	says BLOCK, the report shows all three verdicts plus the policy
138	outcome. Hidden disagreement defeats the purpose of multi-arm
139	dispatch.
140	
141	**Benchmark (prompt or model variant comparison):**
142	
143	- Tabulate the structured output across arms (verdict + findings).
144	- Flag arms that diverged from the others on the verdict line.
145	- Note quality differentials — but do not pick a winner mechanically;
146	  human judgment closes the comparison.
147	
148	**Replay (altered-conditions re-run):**
149	
150	- Compare the new arm against the prior baseline arm directly.
151	- Surface the diff in verdict and findings; do not collapse to a
152	  single "improved / regressed" label.
153	
154	### 6. Render the comparison report
155	
156	Combine arms into a single Markdown report with a row per arm and
157	the structured output side-by-side:
158	
159	```bash
160	cat > "$OUT/report.md" <<EOF
161	# Compare-prompts report
162	
163	- Axis: harness
164	- Prompt: $PROMPT
165	- Profile: smart
166	- Arms: claude, codex, gemini
167	
168	## Per-arm output
169	
170	### claude
171	$(cat "$OUT/claude.md")
172	
173	### codex
174	$(cat "$OUT/codex.md")
175	
176	### gemini
177	$(cat "$OUT/gemini.md")
178	
179	## Aggregation
180	
181	<verdict table + policy outcome>
182	EOF
183	```
184	
185	If the comparison is associated with a bead (e.g., a quorum review
186	of the bead's diff), attach the report as evidence:
187	
188	```bash
189	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body "$OUT/report.md"
190	```
191	
192	## Replacing `--quorum`
193	
194	The retired `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` flag
195	maps to this workflow:
196	
197	| Old | New |
198	|---|---|
199	| `--harnesses=claude,codex,gemini` | three parallel `ddx agent run --harness <name>` calls |
200	| `--quorum=majority` | aggregation step 5, "majority" policy |
201	| `--quorum=unanimous` | aggregation step 5, "unanimous" policy |
202	| inline aggregated stdout | `report.md` rendered by step 6 |
203	
204	The skill formalizes what `--quorum` did inline. Behavioral parity
205	requires choosing the same harness set, the same policy, and routing
206	each arm through `--profile smart` (or whatever profile the original
207	quorum invocation pinned).
208	
209	## Replacing `agent benchmark`
210	
211	The retired `ddx agent benchmark` mapped a prompt across a fixed
212	matrix and rendered a results table. The skill subsumes it by:
213	
214	- Authoring the matrix as the per-arm dispatch list in step 3.
215	- Reusing the output contract in step 2 to keep arms aggregateable.
216	- Rendering the results table in step 6.
217	
218	For benchmarking across many prompts (a prompt suite), see the
219	`benchmark-suite` skill — it composes this skill across a prompt
220	matrix.
221	
222	## Anti-patterns
223	
224	- **Mixing axes silently.** Varying both harness and prompt at once
225	  hides which variable produced the difference. Pick one axis, or
226	  enumerate the full matrix.
227	- **No output contract.** Free-form arm output is not
228	  aggregateable. Always include the structured output contract in
229	  the prompt.
230	- **Dropping failed arms.** A failed arm is data. Mark it as failed
231	  in the report — do not silently aggregate over the survivors.
232	- **Picking a winner mechanically.** Quorum aggregation is
233	  mechanical (policy → verdict). Benchmark aggregation requires
234	  human judgment on quality, not a counting rule.
235	- **One-arm "comparison".** A single dispatch is `ddx agent run`,
236	  not a comparison. This skill requires N ≥ 2.
237	- **Reusing `arm1.md` / `arm2.md` filenames.** Name files for the
238	  varied axis value so the report is self-describing.
239	
240	## CLI reference
241	
242	```bash
243	# Three-harness quorum (majority policy)
244	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/claude.md &
245	ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/codex.md  &
246	ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/gemini.md &
247	wait
248	
249	# Two-prompt benchmark (one harness, varying prompt)
250	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt-v1.md > out/v1.md &
251	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt-v2.md > out/v2.md &
252	wait
253	
254	# Two-persona comparison
255	ddx agent run --harness claude --persona reviewer  --prompt prompt.md > out/reviewer.md  &
256	ddx agent run --harness claude --persona architect --prompt prompt.md > out/architect.md &
257	wait
258	
259	# Attach the comparison report as bead evidence
260	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body out/report.md
261	```
262	
263	## Related skills
264	
265	- `adversarial-review` — N-arm dispatch with adversarial critic
266	  framing layered on top.
267	- `benchmark-suite` — composes `compare-prompts` across a prompt
268	  matrix for suite-level evaluation.
269	- `replay-bead` — re-run a prior bead's prompt under altered
270	  conditions; uses the replay aggregation shape from step 5.
271

1	---
2	name: replay-bead
3	description: Re-run a previously executed bead under altered conditions (different harness, model, profile, persona, or base revision) and diff the new attempt against the prior baseline. Use when investigating why an attempt failed, A/B-testing a model upgrade on a known task, or sanity-checking a regression. Replaces the deprecated `ddx agent replay` CLI.
4	---
5	
6	# Replay Bead
7	
8	A workflow skill for re-running a prior bead attempt under altered
9	conditions and diffing the result against the original baseline.
10	Composes `ddx try` (the layer-2 bead-attempt primitive) with the
11	output structure from `compare-prompts` step 5 (replay aggregation).
12	
13	This skill replaces the retired `ddx agent replay` CLI. The retired
14	command silently re-ran a bead's reconstructed prompt under a swapped
15	harness/model and dropped the result into a sandbox worktree. The
16	skill formalizes the same shape: pin the baseline, vary exactly one
17	condition, capture the new attempt as a peer record, render a diff.
18	
19	## When to use
20	
21	- **Failure investigation** — a bead attempt failed or produced a
22	  poor diff; replay it under a stronger harness or different profile
23	  to see whether the task is tractable at all.
24	- **Model upgrade A/B** — replay a representative bead under the
25	  newer model to see whether the diff materially improves before
26	  rolling the model out as the default.
27	- **Regression sanity check** — replay a previously-green bead under
28	  a candidate change (new prompt, new persona, new profile) to
29	  confirm nothing regressed on a known-good task.
30	- **Base-revision sensitivity** — replay against current HEAD instead
31	  of the original parent revision to see whether intervening commits
32	  changed what the agent produces.
33	
34	For dispatching the *same* prompt across N peers in parallel without
35	a baseline, prefer `compare-prompts`. Replay is specifically a
36	**1-vs-baseline** shape, not an N-arm fan-out.
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### 1. Pin the baseline
41	
42	Identify the prior attempt you are replaying against. Capture:
43	
44	- bead id (`ddx-<short>`)
45	- the attempt's record id (the `<attempt-id>` directory under the
46	  unified run substrate)
47	- the harness, model, profile, persona, and base revision the
48	  baseline ran under
49	- the resulting diff (merged commit, preserved iteration ref, or
50	  no_changes_rationale)
51	
52	```bash
53	BEAD=ddx-abc123
54	BASELINE_ATTEMPT=20260420T101530-aaaaaaaa
55	ddx tries show "$BASELINE_ATTEMPT" > replay/baseline.txt
56	```
57	
58	A replay without a pinned baseline is just a fresh `ddx try` — there
59	is nothing to diff against.
60	
61	### 2. Vary exactly one condition
62	
63	Pick one axis to alter. Mixing axes makes the diff uninterpretable —
64	you cannot tell whether the new model or the new persona drove the
65	difference.
66	
67	| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
68	|---|---|---|
69	| harness | `--harness <other>` | model-class, profile, persona, base rev |
70	| model | `--model <other>` | harness, profile, persona, base rev |
71	| profile | `--profile <other>` | harness, model, persona, base rev |
72	| persona | `--persona <other>` | harness, model, profile, base rev |
73	| base rev | `--from <rev>` (or `--at-head`) | harness, model, profile, persona |
74	| prompt | regenerated bead description / spec | harness, model, profile, persona, base rev |
75	
76	If the comparison genuinely requires varying two axes, run two
77	replays sequentially against the same baseline rather than one replay
78	with two changes — each replay then has a clean 1-vs-baseline diff.
79	
80	### 3. Dispatch the replay attempt
81	
82	Run `ddx try` with the altered condition. The replay must run in an
83	isolated worktree so it cannot stomp the baseline result; `ddx try`
84	defaults to that, but pass `--no-merge` so the replay stays preserved
85	as an iteration ref rather than fast-forwarding into the base branch.
86	
87	```bash
88	REPLAY_OUT=replay-$BEAD-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
89	mkdir -p "$REPLAY_OUT"
90	
91	ddx try "$BEAD" \
92	  --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" \
93	  --harness codex \
94	  --profile smart \
95	  --no-merge \
96	  > "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.log" 2> "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.err"
97	```
98	
99	Notes:
100	
101	- `--from <rev>` pins the base revision to match the baseline. Omit
102	  (or use `--at-head`) only when base-rev is the axis being varied.
103	- `--no-merge` preserves the replay's worktree state as an iteration
104	  ref so its diff can be inspected without disturbing the base
105	  branch. The baseline already shipped (or didn't); the replay is an
106	  observation, not a re-merge.
107	- Capture stderr separately. A replay that errored is a result —
108	  surface it in the report rather than silently dropping it.
109	
110	### 4. Locate the replay's record
111	
112	`ddx try` writes a layer-2 record under the unified run substrate.
113	Capture its attempt id alongside the baseline's:
114	
115	```bash
116	REPLAY_ATTEMPT=$(ddx tries list --bead "$BEAD" --limit 1 --format id)
117	ddx tries show "$REPLAY_ATTEMPT" > "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.txt"
118	```
119	
120	### 5. Diff baseline vs replay
121	
122	The replay aggregation shape is a direct 1-vs-1 diff, not an N-arm
123	roll-up. Surface differences along three dimensions:
124	
125	**Outcome diff:**
126	
127	| Dimension | Baseline | Replay |
128	|---|---|---|
129	| exit | merged / preserved / no_changes | merged / preserved / no_changes |
130	| AC items satisfied | `<n>/<m>` | `<n>/<m>` |
131	| token / duration | from run record | from run record |
132	
133	**Code diff:**
134	
135	```bash
136	git diff "$BASELINE_REF" "$REPLAY_REF" -- > "$REPLAY_OUT/code.diff"
137	```
138	
139	Where `$BASELINE_REF` is the baseline merge commit (or its
140	preserved-iteration ref) and `$REPLAY_REF` is the replay's preserved
141	iteration ref. The code diff is the load-bearing artifact — the
142	verdict is downstream of "what actually changed in the worktree."
143	
144	**Reasoning diff (if available):**
145	
146	If both attempts produced session logs or written rationale, summarize
147	how they differ — but do not let the model self-narrative dominate
148	over the code diff. The code is the ground truth.
149	
150	### 6. Render the replay report
151	
152	```bash
153	cat > "$REPLAY_OUT/report.md" <<EOF
154	# Replay report: $BEAD
155	
156	- Baseline attempt: $BASELINE_ATTEMPT
157	- Replay attempt:   $REPLAY_ATTEMPT
158	- Axis varied:      harness (claude → codex)
159	- Held constant:    profile=smart, persona=<same>, base-rev=<same>
160	
161	## Outcome diff
162	
163	<table from step 5>
164	
165	## Code diff
166	
167	\`\`\`diff
168	$(cat "$REPLAY_OUT/code.diff")
169	\`\`\`
170	
171	## Verdict
172	
173	<improved / regressed / equivalent — human judgment, not mechanical>
174	EOF
175	```
176	
177	If the replay is associated with the bead's evidence trail, attach:
178	
179	```bash
180	ddx bead evidence add "$BEAD" --type replay --body "$REPLAY_OUT/report.md"
181	```
182	
183	Do not collapse the verdict to a single "improved/regressed" label
184	mechanically. Replay verdicts require human judgment on whether the
185	new diff is genuinely better, equivalent under noise, or regressed
186	on a dimension the baseline got right.
187	
188	## Replacing `ddx agent replay`
189	
190	The retired command mapped to this workflow:
191	
192	| Old | New |
193	|---|---|
194	| `ddx agent replay <bead> --harness <h>` | `ddx try <bead> --harness <h> --no-merge` (step 3) |
195	| `ddx agent replay <bead> --model <m>` | `ddx try <bead> --model <m> --no-merge` |
196	| `ddx agent replay <bead> --at-head` | `ddx try <bead> --at-head --no-merge` (varies base-rev axis) |
197	| `ddx agent replay <bead> --sandbox` | always implicit — `ddx try` runs in an isolated worktree |
198	| inline reconstructed prompt | bead description is re-resolved by `ddx try` per layer-2 |
199	| no diff rendering | step 5/6 produce explicit baseline-vs-replay diff |
200	
201	Behavioral parity requires pinning `--from` to the baseline's base
202	revision (the retired command defaulted to "closing commit parent")
203	unless base-rev is the varied axis.
204	
205	## Anti-patterns
206	
207	- **Replay without a pinned baseline.** A replay against nothing is
208	  just a `ddx try` re-attempt; there is no diff and no insight. If
209	  you do not have an attempt id to compare against, you are not
210	  replaying — you are re-trying.
211	- **Varying multiple conditions at once.** A replay that swaps
212	  harness *and* profile cannot attribute the diff to either change.
213	  Run sequential single-axis replays instead.
214	- **Merging the replay.** Replays are observations, not re-merges.
215	  Always pass `--no-merge` so the baseline's already-shipped result
216	  is not silently overwritten by a "better-looking" replay diff.
217	- **Mechanical winner-picking.** "Replay produced more lines /
218	  closed more AC items" is not a verdict. The replay diff might be
219	  larger and worse. Human judgment closes the comparison.
220	- **Comparing only the rationale.** Self-narratives diverge between
221	  attempts even when the code diff is essentially identical. The
222	  code diff is the ground truth; the rationale is supporting
223	  context.
224	- **Skipping `--from`.** Omitting `--from` lets the replay drift to
225	  HEAD; the resulting diff conflates "different agent" with
226	  "different starting state." Pin the base rev unless that *is* the
227	  axis being varied.
228	
229	## CLI reference
230	
231	```bash
232	# Replay under a different harness, baseline-pinned, no merge
233	ddx try "$BEAD" --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" --harness codex --no-merge
234	
235	# Replay under a different model
236	ddx try "$BEAD" --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" --model claude-opus-4-7 --no-merge
237	
238	# Replay against current HEAD (varies the base-rev axis)
239	ddx try "$BEAD" --at-head --no-merge
240	
241	# Inspect baseline and replay records
242	ddx tries show "$BASELINE_ATTEMPT"
243	ddx tries show "$REPLAY_ATTEMPT"
244	
245	# Diff the worktree refs
246	git diff "$BASELINE_REF" "$REPLAY_REF" --
247	
248	# Attach the replay report as bead evidence
249	ddx bead evidence add "$BEAD" --type replay --body replay-out/report.md
250	```
251	
252	## Related skills
253	
254	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm parallel dispatch when there is no
255	  baseline, just a comparison matrix. Replay is its 1-vs-baseline
256	  specialization.
257	- `adversarial-review` — pressure-test the replay's diff with a
258	  critic prompt before accepting "replay improved baseline."
259	- `benchmark-suite` — for replaying many beads under the same
260	  altered condition (e.g., "how does the new model perform across
261	  the last 20 closed beads"), compose this skill across a bead
262	  matrix.
263

input.content:
---
name: benchmark-suite
description: Run a benchmark across a matrix of prompts (a prompt suite) by composing `compare-prompts` per row, then roll the per-prompt comparisons into a suite-level scoreboard. Use when evaluating a model, harness, persona, or profile change against many tasks at once — e.g., "how does codex compare to claude across our 20-prompt review suite," or "did the new persona regress on any task in the eval set." Replaces the deprecated `ddx agent benchmark` CLI when the input is a prompt suite rather than a single prompt.
---

# Benchmark Suite

A workflow skill for running a benchmark across **a matrix of prompts**.
Composes the `compare-prompts` skill (per-prompt N-arm dispatch) across
a prompt suite, then aggregates the per-prompt comparison reports into
a single suite-level scoreboard.

`compare-prompts` answers *"for one prompt, how do N arms differ."*
This skill answers *"across M prompts, how do N arms differ on
average — and on which prompts do they diverge."*

The retired `ddx agent benchmark` CLI handled the single-prompt case
inline; the suite case is this skill.

## When to use

- **Model upgrade evaluation** — score a candidate model against the
  default model across the team's eval prompt suite before flipping
  the default.
- **Harness comparison at scale** — compare `claude` / `codex` /
  `gemini` across a curated set of representative tasks, not just one.
- **Persona regression check** — confirm a refined persona did not
  regress on any prompt in the eval suite before shipping it as the
  new default.
- **Profile A/B at scale** — compare `cheap` vs `smart` profile across
  a representative task suite to quantify the cost/quality tradeoff
  on real workloads.
- **Cost-tiered routing validation** — confirm the cheap-tier model
  matches the strong-tier model on tasks the routing layer expects to
  be cheap-tractable, and only escalates the rest.

For evaluating **one prompt** across N arms, use `compare-prompts`
directly — this skill is N×M overkill for a single row.

For **replaying many beads** under one altered condition, prefer the
matrix shape described in `replay-bead`'s "Related skills" — that is
a bead suite, not a prompt suite.

## Workflow

### 1. Curate the prompt suite

Assemble the prompts that constitute the suite. Each prompt is a
single file with the same **output contract** (per `compare-prompts`
step 2) so per-prompt comparisons are mechanically aggregateable
across the suite.

```
suite/
  prompts/
    01-code-review-easy.md
    02-code-review-hard.md
    03-spec-critique.md
    ...
    20-architecture-question.md
  expected/                # optional: per-prompt expected verdict
    01-code-review-easy.txt
    ...
```

Suite curation rules:

- **Stable prompt ids.** Number-prefixed filenames keep the report
  ordering deterministic. Renaming a prompt mid-suite invalidates
  prior runs of the suite.
- **One output contract for the whole suite.** Mixed contracts make
  the suite-level scoreboard incoherent. If two prompts genuinely
  need different output shapes, they belong to different suites.
- **Cover representative difficulty.** A suite of only easy prompts
  hides where arms diverge; a suite of only hard prompts hides where
  arms agree. Mix.
- **Optional baselines.** If the suite has known-good answers (golden
  outputs or expected verdicts), keep them under `expected/` so the
  suite-level report can grade arms against ground truth, not just
  against each other.

### 2. Define the comparison axis

Pick exactly one axis to vary across arms (per `compare-prompts`
step 1). The same axis is held constant **across the entire suite** —
the suite varies the prompt; the row varies the arm.

| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
|---|---|---|
| harness | `--harness claude` vs `--harness codex` vs `--harness gemini` | profile, persona, model-class |
| model | `--model <a>` vs `--model <b>` | harness, profile, persona |
| profile | `--profile cheap` vs `--profile smart` | harness, persona |
| persona | `--persona reviewer` vs `--persona architect` | harness, profile |

Mixing axes mid-suite (codex+smart on prompts 1–10, claude+cheap on
prompts 11–20) makes the scoreboard uninterpretable. Pick one axis
and run the full matrix.

### 3. Dispatch per prompt (compose `compare-prompts`)

For each prompt in the suite, run the `compare-prompts` workflow.
The per-prompt output is a `compare-prompts` report; the suite is the
collection of those reports.

```bash
SUITE=suite/prompts
ARMS="claude codex gemini"
PROFILE=smart
OUT=benchmark-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
mkdir -p "$OUT/per-prompt"

for prompt in "$SUITE"/*.md; do
  pid=$(basename "$prompt" .md)
  pdir="$OUT/per-prompt/$pid"
  mkdir -p "$pdir"

  for arm in $ARMS; do
    ddx agent run --harness "$arm" --profile "$PROFILE" \
      --prompt "$prompt" \
      > "$pdir/$arm.md" 2> "$pdir/$arm.err" &
  done
  wait
done
```

Concurrency notes:

- **Within a prompt:** dispatch arms in parallel (the `&` + `wait` in
  the inner loop). This is `compare-prompts` step 3.
- **Across prompts:** default to sequential, not parallel. A 20-prompt
  suite × 3-arm matrix at once is 60 concurrent agent invocations,
  which will saturate provider rate limits, distort token/duration
  measurements, and make a single transient failure cascade across
  unrelated rows.
- For large suites, batch with bounded parallelism (e.g., 2–4
  prompts in flight at a time) rather than full fan-out. Cost-tiered
  routing especially benefits from sequential prompts so per-row
  cost numbers are clean.

### 4. Verify each (prompt, arm) cell produced output

Per `compare-prompts` step 4, but applied across the full matrix.
A failed cell is data — record the failure reason; do not silently
omit it from the scoreboard.

```bash
for pdir in "$OUT"/per-prompt/*/; do
  for arm in $ARMS; do
    if [ ! -s "$pdir/$arm.md" ]; then
      echo "CELL FAILED: $(basename "$pdir") / $arm" >> "$OUT/failures.log"
    fi
  done
done
```

Cells that errored or returned empty appear in the scoreboard as
`FAIL`, not as a missing entry. A scoreboard with hidden gaps
mis-attributes wins to whichever arm happened to fail less often.

### 5. Aggregate per prompt

For each prompt, run the `compare-prompts` step-5 aggregation against
its arms. The per-prompt aggregate is the row in the suite scoreboard.

```bash
for pdir in "$OUT"/per-prompt/*/; do
  pid=$(basename "$pdir")
  # Per-prompt report uses the same shape as compare-prompts step 6.
  # The suite-level aggregator below reads its verdict line.
  cat > "$pdir/report.md" <<EOF
# $pid

## Per-arm output

$(for arm in $ARMS; do
    echo "### $arm"; echo
    cat "$pdir/$arm.md" 2>/dev/null || echo "FAIL"; echo
  done)

## Aggregation

<verdict per arm + cross-arm divergence note>
EOF
done
```

Aggregation policy at the row level depends on what the suite is for:

- **Quorum suite** (consensus check across arms): each row's
  aggregate is `unanimous` / `majority` / `split` per
  `compare-prompts` step 5. The suite-level scoreboard tallies how
  often the arms agreed.
- **Benchmark suite** (quality comparison): each row tabulates the
  per-arm verdict. The suite-level scoreboard surfaces divergence
  patterns — *not* a mechanical winner per row.
- **Graded suite** (arms vs expected ground truth): each row scores
  each arm against `expected/<pid>.txt`. The suite-level scoreboard
  shows pass-rate per arm.

### 6. Roll up to the suite-level scoreboard

The scoreboard is the load-bearing artifact. It is a matrix of
arms × prompts plus marginal summaries.

```
| Prompt                    | claude  | codex   | gemini  |
|---------------------------|---------|---------|---------|
| 01-code-review-easy       | APPROVE | APPROVE | APPROVE |
| 02-code-review-hard       | APPROVE | BLOCK   | APPROVE |
| 03-spec-critique          | BLOCK   | BLOCK   | APPROVE |
| ...                       | ...     | ...     | ...     |
| 20-architecture-question  | APPROVE | APPROVE | FAIL    |
|---------------------------|---------|---------|---------|
| Pass rate (vs expected)   | 18/20   | 16/20   | 17/20   |
| Mean duration (s)         | 12.4    | 18.9    | 9.2     |
| Mean tokens (in/out)      | …       | …       | …       |
| Cost ($)                  | …       | …       | …       |
| Cells failed              | 0       | 0       | 1       |
```

Render in Markdown for the report and emit the same data as JSON for
downstream tooling (cost-tiered routing decisions, dashboards,
follow-up beads).

```bash
cat > "$OUT/scoreboard.md" <<EOF
# Benchmark suite scoreboard

- Suite: $SUITE
- Axis: harness
- Arms: $ARMS
- Profile: $PROFILE
- Prompts: $(ls "$SUITE" | wc -l)
- Run: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

<scoreboard table>

## Per-prompt detail

$(for pdir in "$OUT"/per-prompt/*/; do
    pid=$(basename "$pdir")
    echo "- [$pid]($pdir/report.md)"
  done)

## Failures

$(cat "$OUT/failures.log" 2>/dev/null || echo "(none)")
EOF
```

### 7. Surface divergence and cost, not just a winner

Two things matter at suite level beyond "which arm scored highest":

- **Where do arms diverge?** Rows where all arms agree are uninformative
  noise; rows where arms disagree are where the comparison actually
  pays off. Highlight the divergent rows in a dedicated section so the
  reader sees them without scanning the whole matrix.
- **Cost vs quality.** Marginal cost per arm × pass rate is the
  cost-tiered routing input. A cheap arm that passes 17/20 at 1/10th
  the price of a strong arm passing 19/20 is a routing decision, not
  a "loser." Show both columns; let the human close the call.

A suite-level verdict ("codex wins") that hides divergence patterns
or cost asymmetries is worse than no verdict.

### 8. Attach to evidence (optional)

If the suite was run in service of a specific decision (model
upgrade, persona ship, profile default flip), attach the scoreboard
to the relevant bead's evidence:

```bash
ddx bead evidence add <decision-bead-id> --type benchmark \
  --body "$OUT/scoreboard.md"
```

The per-prompt reports stay under `$OUT/per-prompt/` as referenced
detail; the scoreboard is the summary.

## Replacing `ddx agent benchmark`

The retired CLI mapped to two distinct shapes. Per-prompt benchmarks
went to `compare-prompts`. Suite-level benchmarks come here:

| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `ddx agent benchmark --prompt p.md --harnesses=a,b,c` | `compare-prompts` (single row, N arms) |
| `ddx agent benchmark --suite suite/ --harnesses=a,b,c` | this skill (M rows × N arms) |
| inline tabular stdout | `scoreboard.md` rendered by step 6 |
| implicit "winner" verdict | step 7 — divergence + cost, not a single winner |

Behavioral parity requires the suite curation rules in step 1 (stable
ids, one output contract) and the step-2 axis lock — `--harnesses=a,b`
on a suite is a single-axis (harness) suite-level benchmark.

## Anti-patterns

- **Mixed output contracts within a suite.** Aggregation breaks if
  prompt 03 returns a verdict line and prompt 04 returns a JSON blob.
  Either normalize the contracts or split into two suites.
- **Mid-suite axis change.** Switching harness or profile partway
  through invalidates the scoreboard — every cell must run under the
  same arm definition. Re-run the whole suite if the axis shifted.
- **Hidden cell failures.** A 19/20 score that secretly excluded the
  one prompt where the candidate arm crashed is a misleading 19/20.
  `FAIL` is a cell value, not a missing row.
- **Mechanical "winner" verdict.** The scoreboard surfaces signal;
  the decision (ship the candidate / hold / route by cost tier) is
  human judgment on quality + cost + divergence pattern.
- **Suite of one prompt.** If M = 1, this is `compare-prompts`. The
  suite shape only earns its weight when M ≥ ~5.
- **Renaming prompts between runs.** Stable prompt ids are how
  longitudinal suite results stay comparable. Treat the suite layout
  as a versioned input.
- **Full N×M parallelism.** Saturates rate limits, distorts duration
  measurements, and turns one transient hiccup into a wave of
  unrelated cell failures. Bounded per-prompt parallelism only.

## CLI reference

```bash
# Suite layout
suite/prompts/01-*.md … 20-*.md
suite/expected/01-*.txt … 20-*.txt   # optional ground truth

# Per-prompt N-arm dispatch (compose compare-prompts)
for prompt in suite/prompts/*.md; do
  pid=$(basename "$prompt" .md)
  mkdir -p "out/per-prompt/$pid"
  for arm in claude codex gemini; do
    ddx agent run --harness "$arm" --profile smart --prompt "$prompt" \
      > "out/per-prompt/$pid/$arm.md" 2> "out/per-prompt/$pid/$arm.err" &
  done
  wait
done

# Inspect a single row's compare-prompts report
cat out/per-prompt/02-code-review-hard/report.md

# Suite-level scoreboard
cat out/scoreboard.md

# Attach as evidence on the decision bead
ddx bead evidence add ddx-<id> --type benchmark --body out/scoreboard.md
```

## Related skills

- `compare-prompts` — single-prompt N-arm dispatch. This skill
  composes it once per row.
- `replay-bead` — 1-vs-baseline replay of a single bead under altered
  conditions; the bead-suite analogue (replay across many beads under
  one condition) reuses this skill's matrix shape with bead ids in
  place of prompt files.
- `adversarial-review` — pressure-test the suite's per-row outputs
  with a critic prompt before accepting a candidate arm.
- `effort-estimate` — cost projection for a candidate suite run
  before dispatching M × N invocations.
